symptom:
AVD session host shows shut
down status though it is running issue.
rootCause:
Troubleshooting
done:   1.First
we check the system event log to see if the machine has restart record or not. If the machine has not been started for over
90 days, then it is a normal behavior as RDCB will clear the record.  ->Checked, no issue.   2.As
per our checking, we found there should be some agents missing in the target
host.      We only have Remote Desktop Agent Boot
Loader & Infrastructure Agent & WebRTC Redirector Services installed on
the issued host.           Expected:         2.Ran
command dism /online /cleanup-image /restorehealth and scf /cannow, got the
following error:     Error: 0x800f081f   3.
When we tried to check the event log from backend, we found therere no events
related to MSIInstaller and WVD-agent since 4/5/2024.      Its strange since normally there is URL
checking event recorded and new package downloaded when latest version is
detected.      From backend side, we found the current
RDAgentBootLoader version is 1.0.8297.800, actually the latest RDAgent version
is 1.0.8431.2300      And the OS version is lower than other
working session hosts in the same host pool.    4.After
we register a VM to a host pool within the Azure Virtual Desktop service, the
agent regularly refreshes the VM's token whenever the VM is active.       The
certificate for the registration token is valid for 90 days. Because of this
90-day limit, we recommend VMs to be online for 20 minutes every 90 days so
that the machine can refresh its tokens and update the agent and side-by-side
stack components.       Turning
on the VM within this time limit prevents its registration token from expiring
or becoming invalid.       If we started your VM after 90 days and
are experiencing registration issues, follow the instructions in the Azure
Virtual Desktop agent troubleshooting guide to remove the VM from the host
pool, reinstall the agent, and reregister it to the pool.   5.
Then we tried to reinstall the RD Agent Boot Loader. Then the azure vm has been
added to the host pool.      But in the registry: Computer\HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\RDInfraAgent,
lots of registry keys are missing. Especially IsRegistered.      The expected display: below screenshot is from testing lab environment:          6.Then
we collected process monitor log for installing RD Agent Boot Loader process. Compare the good scenario and fail scenario.   6.1
The RDAgentBootLoader.exe launched is a 32bit version.          This is the reason why it tries to
access the registry "HKLM\SOFTWARE\WOW6432Node\Microsoft\RDAgentBootLoader"
and cannot find it.          12:07:08.6109265
PM        RDAgentBootLoader.exe        4704        6008        Process
Start                0.0000000        SUCCESS        Parent
PID: 912, Command line: "C:\Program Files\Microsoft
RDInfra\RDAgentBootLoader_1.0.8925.0\RDAgentBootLoader.exe", Current
directory: C:\Windows\system32\, Environment:           12:07:17.7408945
PM        RDAgentBootLoader.exe        4704        6792        RegOpenKey        HKLM\SOFTWARE\WOW6432Node\Microsoft\RDAgentBootLoader        0.0000086        NAME NOT FOUND        Desired
Access:
Read/Write        0        NT
AUTHORITY\SYSTEM        "C:\Program
Files\Microsoft
RDInfra\RDAgentBootLoader_1.0.8925.0\RDAgentBootLoader.exe"   6.2
The file "C:\Program Files\Microsoft
RDInfra\RDAgentBootLoader_1.0.8925.0\RDAgentBootLoader.exe" is
created by msiexec.exe. The 32bit seems no difference.     Non-working:      12:06:56.0725504
PM        msiexec.exe        8796        1592        FASTIO_QUERY_INFORMATION        C:\Program
Files\Microsoft
RDInfra\RDAgentBootLoader_1.0.8925.0\RDAgentBootLoader.exe        0.0000049        SUCCESS        Type:
QueryStandardInformationFile, AllocationSize: 45,056, EndOfFile: 44,824,
NumberOfLinks: 1, DeletePending: False, Directory:
False        0        NT
AUTHORITY\SYSTEM        C:\Windows\system32\msiexec.exe
/V   Working:   10:25:17.6553130
AM        msiexec.exe        4220        6316        FASTIO_QUERY_INFORMATION        C:\Program
Files\Microsoft
RDInfra\RDAgentBootLoader_1.0.8925.0\RDAgentBootLoader.exe        0.0000046        SUCCESS        Type:
QueryStandardInformationFile, AllocationSize: 45,056, EndOfFile: 44,824,
NumberOfLinks: 1, DeletePending: False, Directory:
False        0        NT
AUTHORITY\SYSTEM        C:\Windows\system32\msiexec.exe
/V        It means the AnyCPU RDAgentBootLoader.exe is
forced to run as 32bit on customer's machine.   In
local environment, it runs as 64bit.       We need to check customer's environment
and file directly by corflags.exe and ldr64.exe     Cause: We
checked customer's machine. Their global Enable64Bit setting is 0. This causes
the AnyCPU .NET application runs as 32bit and causes problem. Actually
we installed the .msix 64bit agent.  no issue for the .msix package.   But
the cx machine has configured only 32 bit .net program to run. It
causes it tries to read the 32 bit related path instead of the 64 bit path. But
name not found. Thus it will not continue.   Customer
side: Run CorFlags.exe    The PE32 format stands for Portable Executable
32-bit, while PE32+ is Portable Executable 64-bit format.   C:\temp\Tools>Ldr64.exe query loading
kernel32...done. retrieved
GetComPlusPackageInstallStatus entry point retrieved
SetComPlusPackageInstallStatus entry point Current
status is: 0x00000000
solution:
To
fix the issue, we need to set the Enable64Bit back to default by running: Ldr64.exe set64 Then
we try reinstall the RD Agent Boot Loader. Then the Azure VM has been
registered to the AVD host pool successfully. Check
all components are installed successfully.

(microsoft.com)  Issue repro ways: Issue is reproducible connecting via AVD desktop client. Issue is NOT reproducible connecting via web client (HTML). Issue is reproducible on direct RDP (mstsc) when 'Intel Wireless Bluetooth' is checked under RemoteFx USB devices:

Do not redirect the Intel Wireless Bluetooth on the AVD session:Peripheral and resource redirection over the Remote Desktop Protocol | Microsoft Learn Detailed steps for same:  Get the Class Guid from Device manager for Intel

"*$deviceClassGuid*" -and $_.InstanceId -like "USB\*" -and

and resource redirection over the Remote Desktop Protocol | Microsoft Learn   After following the above steps, you should be able to connect to the AVD session via desktop client and you can select the devices needs to be redirected by clicking on marked option below:

symptom:
Issue:
 AVD session hosts are in Unavailable Health state. Domain join and domain trust check
 fails.  
 
  
  
   
   
   
   
   
   
   
   
   
   
   
   
  
  
  
 
  
  
 Symptoms: Health
 check failure shows Domain join and domain trust check fails. 
  This health check verifies that the session
 host is joined to a domain. If this check fails, it is fatal, and no
 connections will succeed. To solve this, join the virtual machine to a domain. 
 
  
  
   
   
   
 Environment: 
 Azure Virtual Desktop 
 Entra
 ID joined VMs 
 User
 identity: Hybrid - which
 are on-premises AD DS identities that are synced to Microsoft Entra ID. 
 Host pool type: Pooled 
 OS: Windows
 10, Windows 11 and Windows Server 2022 session hosts. 
  
 Output C:\Users\DefaultAdmin>dsregcmd /status  
 
  
  
 From the logs we can see
 that the VM is not joined to the Azure AD domain.
rootCause:
The error
message suggests that there's a naming conflict related to the hostnames of
your Azure Virtual Machines (VMs) when trying to join them to Azure Active
Directory (AAD). It is caused by the previous registration. The issue here is
with Resource Cleanup. Sometimes, when VMs are destroyed and recreated,
there can be delays in the release of hostnames, especially if soft-deletion is
enabled for resources. Ensure that you have completely removed any previous VM
instances and associated resources (NICs, storage, etc.) before deploying new
VMs with the same host names.   On the Session
host, AADLoginForWindowsExtension log file under File Location:
C:\WindowsAzure\Logs\Plugins\Microsoft.Azure.ActiveDirectory.AADLoginForWindows\1.0.0.1 AADLoginForWindowsExtension
logs shows the hostname conflict, see the below error.   "message":{"lang":"en","value":"Another object with the same
value for property hostnames already
exists."},"requestId":"*********-29f9-42da-b4d2-*********","date":"2024-06-18T08:34:25","details":[{"code":"ObjectConflict","target":"hostnames","message":"Another
object with the same value for property hostnames already
exists."},{"code":"ConflictingObjects","target":"Device_******-17d9-41e3-be75-7c401******","message":"Another
object with the same value for property hostnames already exists."}]}}   [{"status":{"code":-2145648509,"formattedMessage":{"lang":"en-US","message":"AAD
Join failed with status code: -2145648509. AzureSecureVMJoinOperation:
DeviceEnroller::AzureSecureVMEnroll failed 0x801c0083. The hostname is already used by another device in
this tenant, please change the VM name to redeploy the extension."}
solution:
Please verify that there is a
stale Device ID (as below) in EntraID Tenant>Devices>All Devices 9******-681a-4d92-ac2f-******* Delete that device from the Entra
ID  Attempt to join the VM � dv*-dp-***-05 to the
Entra ID          
    Command: dsregcmd /join  Verify if the device has joined
successfully          
      Command: dsregcmd /status.  Restart the Session host   Once the device is joined to the
Entra ID domain the Domain check Health check will succeed and the SH will
become available in the Host pool.   You can replicate the same steps for
the other impacted VMs.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#196093

symptom:
Naming convention with capital letters showing
lowercase in host pool and Resource Group.
rootCause:
Customer has
deployed resource groups and host pool using a naming convention with capital
letters. However, when they check in the AVD host pool blade, they appear in
lowercase. Upon clicking and opening, they display in capital letters again and
customer was experiencing the issue right from the beginning.
solution:
Business impact: They are in the process of importing all resources
into Terraform, and this inconsistency is causing issues with the import
process, prompting DevOps to attempt replacements. This discrepancy is causing
our DevOps pipeline to fail during the import process, putting our import
projects on hold.    We have also noticed that the host pool and resource
group names randomly change to lowercase only in the host pool blade. When we
manually check in the resource group or through PowerShell output, everything
appears correct in capital letters:      Scoping:   
 Does the Resource Group naming
     convention also change?  - Yes, sometimes the resource group or host
     pool name, but occasionally both. 
 Does the RG/HP name change when
     we do a refresh?  - No, sometimes it get reverted back after few
     hours and goes back in another few hours 
 What happens if we use
     PowerShell to query the Resource Group/hostpool name�does it show in
     lowercase and uppercase? � PowerShell output is showing correctly but
     portal is showing incorrect value. Refer screenshots below 
 Does the naming convention
     change only when auto scale is enabled on the dedicated hostpool, or do
     other naming conventions also change where auto scale is disabled?  -
     Not 100% sure the link between autoscale, but noticed mainly on autoscale
     enabled pools.            But in the AVD host pool resource ID, it is
incorrectly displayed in lowercase. This discrepancy is causing our DevOps
pipeline to fail during the import process, putting our import projects on
hold. Sample Case : 1, Resource Group Name   In AVD, Resource Group showing in lowercase:    Resource ID of host pool also showing the resource
group name in smaller case    But the resource group from the RG blade is in
capital. (the actual value)      Resource ID of resource group also showing Capital
letter  Sample 2 : Host pool letters   Host Pool : Main hostpool page showing as small
letters    Same Host pool : Hostpool name showing capital letter.
Changed automatically     Error in Pipeline while import PR is trying to delete lowercase RG and replace with
capital letter!      Resolution: we engaged PG as discussed internally to check what is
causing the problem: Incident-523108652
Details - IcM (microsofticm.com)   They
confirmed that the issue is by design, providing the following explanation:   "Please do not expect the casing to match
exactly. These are non-deterministic, and from the user point of view, you
should be ignoring the casing when trying to compare the strings. Sometimes,
even RPs may send with inconsistent casing as well, so this is expected. This
is by design, and there's no mitigation as part of the incident process that
can be applied to resolve this incident asap. Please don't rely on casing and
always use ignore case in your scripts when doing string comparison."   If you
encounter similar issues, please inform the customer about this design
behavior, and advise them to ignore the casing when performing string
comparisons.

        Log Name: ApplicationSource: Application ErrorDate: 18/10/2024 19:00:57Event ID: 1000Task Category: Application Crashing EventsLevel: ErrorKeywords: ClassicUser: N/AComputer: XXXXXXDescription:Faulting application name: svchost.exe_AppXSvc , version: 10.0.19041.4355, time stamp: 0x9ce47784Faulting module name: appxdeploymentserver.dll, version: 10.0.19041.4894, time stamp: 0xd3d51e5cException code: 0xc0000005 <----- <----- <-----Fault offset: 0x00000000002f7809.Faulting process id: 0x67f4Faulting application start time: 0x01db217c194d60e8.Faulting application path: C:\Windows\system32\svchost.exeFaulting module path: c:\windows\system32\appxdeploymentserver.dllReport Id: 33b492bc-23cb-46bd-be4a-4f3d2000de8e.
rootCause:
The AppXSvc is crashing. Do you know what it is? Lets ask our good friend Copilot: 
    
       
    
      
    <Copilot>
    
      
    AppXSvc, or the AppX Deployment Service, is responsible for deploying store apps on Windows. Issues with AppXSvc can cause significant problems, such as user logons hanging at a 
    black screen
     for up to 5 minutes and the start menu becoming unresponsive. These issues are often related to deployment requests getting stuck in the AppXSvc queue
    .
     
    
      
    </Copilot>
    
      
    
      
    As you can see, during the logon, we will try to install / register modern apps for the user. So, if AppXSvc is having a problem, chances are high we end up with a Black screen. 
    
      
    
      
    <Background information on the logon process for the user>
    
      
    If you do want to learn more about the logon process of the user, I can highly recommend the following 
    GEARS
    -Sessions that provides you with a deep dive on Cloud Academy (you do need to login) on this very interesting topic as part of the Windows Performance Foundation: Shell training path: 
    
      
    
      Windows Performance(Perf) Foundation: Shell (Archive) | QA Platform
    
  
  
    I do recommend the following 2 sessions which focusing on the 
    logon process
     for the user
    
      
    
      WIN-35P: Crackin' the Shell - Explorer Initialization, the Desktop, Notifications and Action Center part 1 | QA Platform
    
    
      
    
      WIN-36P - Crackin' the Shell - Explorer Initialization, the Desktop, Notifications and Action Center part 2 | QA Platform
    
    
      
    
      
    But I also recommend to check out the entire learning path as it does contain useful information. 
    
      
    </Background information on the logon process for the user>
    
      
    
      
    So, getting back to our 
    Black screen 
    issue. This is a known issue and documented externally here: 
    
      
    
      https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/release-health/status-windows-10-22h2#3421msgdesc
    
    
      
    
      
    And internally here: 
    
      
    
      Servicing: 7D.24: AppXSvc crashes causing black screens on Win10 22H2(ICM 548380823) (KIR)
    
  
  
    It offers you three workarounds: 
    
      
    a.) Install 
    K
    nown 
    I
    ssue 
    R
    ollback (KIR)
    
      
    b.) Install 10D (which will be released 
    today
     (22
    
      nd
    
     of October)) or 11B (which is scheduled for 12
    
      th
    
     of November)
    
      
    c.) Rollback to 7B (which we do not recommend as we would miss out 3 months of security patches)
    
      
    
      
    So, lets talk a bit about KIR: 
    
      
    If I ask 
    CoPilot
    , I am getting the following response: 
    
      
    <Copilot>
    
      
    Known Issue Rollback (KIR) is a Windows Servicing feature that allows for the enabling or disabling of a fix without removing an update. KIR is typically used for non-security code changes contained in Windows Updates. These changes are shipped enabled by default but can be disabled if they introduce a regression. KIRs are managed through local or domain group policy, which configures a registry setting. The KIR activation process involves generating OS-version-specific MSI files that install ADMX files to configure group policy settings.
     
    
      
    </Copilot>
    
      
    
      
    <Background Information on KIR>
    
      
    We do release several changes as part of our updates. Some of those changes, mostly not security related, are marked as Opt-in or Opt-out for six months, so customers can decide if they want to enable/disable that specific update before it becomes generally activated. One way to accomplish that is our 
    KIR
    -Process. 
    
      
    
      
    When we move forward with the KIR, we offer an ADMX that you can install. Once installed, you will find the policy in the policy definition folder of Windows: 
    
      
    C:\Windows\PolicyDefinitions
    
      
    
      
    If you then open the local group policy editor, you will find the KIR-Policy here: 
    
      
    
      
    
      
    
    
      
    So, in this scenario, for KB5040525, we choose to opt-out of the changes introduced by KB5040525 by setting the policy to disable as documented in 
    
      https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/release-health/status-windows-10-22h2#3421msgdesc
    
    . 
    
      
    
      
    But what does this mean? Lets try to explain 
    KIR
     briefly with a flow chart with a simplified approach:
    
      
    
      
    We do three steps (Step 1, 2, 3) during an operation. We start with Step 1, which is identical for both code bases. Due to an update, 
    Step 2
     has CHANGED and is now running a different (updated) code base. If we identify that this is causing issues, our PG can release a KIR to offer you the choice to 
    go back 
    to the 
    OLD CODE BASE 
    for Step 2. Step 3 is again the same for both code bases.
     
    
      
     
    
      
     
    
      
    
      
    
      
    
    
      
    
      
    This is accomplished via a special Policy, which can be deployed using local policies or even domain policies. So, once a KIR is deployed and applied, we check it and based on the configuration we will then revert to the old code base if required. 
  
  
    Keep in mind that KIRs are normally well tested and safe. However, depending on the size of the change, 
    KIR
     can introduce you with 
    side effects
    . 
    
      
    
      
    So, when utilizing KIR, make sure to evaluate it before you plan to roll out through your entire configuration. KIR will require you to reboot the device after the policy has been activated. 
  
  
    If you want to learn more about KIR, please see 
    
      Windows Servicing: Understanding Known Issue Rollbacks (KIR)
    
    . 
    
      
    
      
    and 
    
      
    
      KIR - Known Issue Rollback - Overview
    
  
  
    In addition, KIRs are not designed to be a permanent solution and we will be salvaged once we have released the permanent fix via the 
    KIR_Cleanup
     TAG during an update. Once we public announce the resolution, you still want to disable the policy that was activating the KIR. 
    
      
    </Background Information on KIR>
    
      
     
    
      
    As we have talked about potential side effects, we are also seeing issues with modern Apps in Azure AVD as the AAD Broker is getting stuck. You can identify this by the following event ID: 
    
      
    
      
    
      
    
    
      
    
      
    Event as text: 
    
      
    Log Name:      Application
  
  
    Source:        Microsoft-Windows-AppModel-State
  
  
    Date:          21/10/2024 11:21:57
  
  
    Event ID:      10
  
  
    Task Category: None
  
  
    Level:         Error
  
  
    Keywords:      
  
  
    User:         
    <USER SID>
  
  
    Computer:     xxx
  
  
    Description:
  
  
    Failure to load the application settings for package Microsoft.AAD.BrokerPlugin_cw5n1h2txyewy. Error Code: 
    -2147024893
  
  
    
      
    This error code 
    -2147024893
     translate to ERROR_PATH_NOT_FOUND as you can see on the error translator page at 
    
      https://aka.ms/errors
    
    . 
    
      
    
      
    
      
    
    
      Or you can use the command line version of err.exe which can be found here: Download err.exe: https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/download/details.aspx?id=100432 But what is the AAD Broker? Lets ask again our friend CoPilot: 
    
      
    <CoPilot>
    
      
    The AAD Broker plug-in, also known as Microsoft.AAD.BrokerPlugin.exe, is a component that initiates connections for Azure Active Directory (AAD) operations. 
    
      
    </CoPilot>
  
  
    So, without AAD Broker, we will not be able to authenticate against Azure Active Directory which will have a massive impact on modern apps like Office365 / OneDrive. 
    
      
    
      
    Based on our initial research, we identified a deadlock in the AppXSvc.
    
      
    
      
    Yes, this is the same service that was crashing when we hit the regression in 7D which is documented in our Known issue section for Windows 10  22H2. Once that fix is applied, chances that we hit the Event ID 10 issues are increased as we do not restart the AppXSvc. This is then increasing the chance of getting stuck in the deadlock. 
    
      
    
      
    We are currently investigating this, and as part of the investigation we have created an internal KB for it. 
    
      
    
      PERF: AVD-session host logon black screen + Office SSO failures connection caused by TokenBroker service waiting on AppXSvc to complete the registration of AAD deadlock on Window 10 22H2 multi
    
    
      Action plan to collect data on Event ID 10:  
 
  
  Action plan 
  
 
 
  
  
   
     Increase Size of AppxDeploymentServer
        Operational Event log
                  
    Use this TSS
        command to start after the system got started and it will wait for event ID 10:  
   
   .\TSS.ps1 -PRF_Appx -WaitEvent
   Evt:10:'Application':5:0:AAD/cw5n1h2txyewy:True:OR -NoXperf -NoXray 
     
   It
   will only stop if we hit EventID 10 and event ID 10 does contain AAD or cw5n1h2txyewy 
   Data collection should be less than 200MB per hour 
     
  
  
   
    
      
    However, as we are just at the start of the troubleshooting journey, I will talk more about it in a further ShowCaseCSS as this ShowCaseCSS  is focusing on the black screen.
solution: Apply 10D on Windows 10 22H2 AVD via https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/topic/october-22-2024-kb5045594-os-build-19045-5073-preview-f307a4b0-f62d-4c28-9062-44207aea55c3Please remember to code case against https://microsoft.visualstudio.com/OS/_workitems/edit/53850043 if 10D has resolved the issueIn addition, note that it appears that this also helps a little with the second issue (PERF: AVD-session host logon black screen + Office SSO failures connection caused by TokenBroker service waiting on AppXSvc to complete the registration of AAD deadlock on Window 10 22H2 multi) but we are still seeing some incidents. If you want to help, use the Action plan above to collect data. ICM for the second issue: Incident-560255666 Details - IcM

rootCause:
Issue is
caused by RemoteApp application groups that are in different resource groups

symptom: Abstract You are running Windows 11 22H2 (Nickel) host pool.  Randomly, the users will no longer be able to connect to via Remote Desktop Services.    Symptoms Applications hosted on AVD are stuck on loading. RDP session will get it stuck on Welcome screen with Spining dots forever. No error messages on any of the connections. Existing AVD and RDP Sessions will continue to work normally.
rootCause: This condition occurs because of a deadlock generated by a UDP Endpoint that was abnormally terminated days/hours before the issue starts exhibiting and a trigger of Winstation registry key change via Registry watcher callback in Local Session Manager.  LSM detects a change in the Winstations registry keys, this registry key holds the Remote Desktop Listeners (RDP, Citrix, Vmware, AVD, etc...). Due to this detected change LSM will request Remote Connection Manager to check all listeners to determine which one should be started or not. There are 2 listeners for the AVD-SXS stack, the most recent (n) and n-1 for rollback purposes. The n-1 listener is set to be disabled via registry key (Example: rdp-sxs240705700\fEnableWinStation � DWORD � 0), however, the listener is currently in use. As a result, Termservice will call into the respective component (RDPCoreCDV.dll) to stop all activity and cleanup. One step of the cleanup process is to close all UDP endpoints, so RDPCoreCDV will issue a call to terminate those UDP endpoints (RdpCoreCDV!CRdpUdpLocalEndpoint::TerminateInstance). Unfortunately, if there is another thread that is already in the process of terminating an UDP endpoint that was already closed, the respective thread will remain stuck waiting on itself and blocking the Listener from terminating. This is a deadlock in RDPCoreCDV.dll and the issue is investigated on the ICM and Bug.
solution: Temporary mitigation Reboot solves the issue  The fix has been implemented in the 25H1 release of AVD code: Bug 56611150: Deadlock in RdpCoreCDV!CRdpUdpLocalEndpoint::TerminateInstance on UDP cleanup  For an exact date please consult with an AVD SME since the AVD Update cycle is different from Windows.  Other References: Incident 597759229 : AVD selfhost users unable to log onto VM stuck in winlogon at Welcome due UDP stack cleanupBug 54536096: AVD selfhost users unable to log onto VM stuck in winlogon at Welcome due UDP stack cleanup

file: c:\symbols\mlx5.sys\5AEEBA601a4000\mlx5.sys    Image path: \SystemRoot\System32\drivers\mlx5.sys    Image name: mlx5.sys    Browse all global symbols  functions  data  Symbol Reload    Timestamp:        Sun May  6 10:18:40 2018 (5AEEBA60) We asked the customer to update the driver; however, we received a reply that they did not install it separately and is included as part of Azure Marketplace image.
rootCause: After engaging the Azure team, we noted that the driver is linked to the Accelerated Networking:    Note on Accelerated Networking: The Mellanox/Accelerated Networking driver is a virtual network device optimized specifically for the virtualized environment rather than emulating a physical network adapter. Instead of pretending to be a real hardware network card, the virtual device �knows� it�s running inside a virtual machine and can more directly interact with the hypervisor. This more direct interaction typically results in greater efficiency and better performance, such as lower overhead and reduced latency compared to emulated network adapters. However, due to this interaction, it is important that the OS-level driver is up to date to maintain optimal performance and stability of the Guest OS.   -Analyzing the client's configuration, we observed the AN is disabled:      -But from another view (DRI dashboard) it looks like as enabled:      We received the following information from Azure PG:  This VM size Standard (Edsv5) actually requires Accelerated Networking so we were observing behavior by design. There are pending documentation updates for https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/sizes/memory-optimized/edsv5-series?tabs=sizebasic  to clarify that AN is required.  There are plans to change the Portal/API to not allow the customer to disable AN on these SKUs (Stock Keeping Unit).  The VM family EDSv5-series is placed on hardware clusters where accelerated networking functionality cannot be fully toggled off at the host level. Disabling Accelerated Networking from the portal/CLI/Powershell will update the VM schema, but the actual cluster serving this workload is Overlake/Accelerated Networking powered.  Customers may report that accelerated networking has been disabled through the Azure portal, Azure CLI, or PowerShell. We see that Accelerated Networking is visible as disabled on the Azure Portal/VM schema/on ASC. However, within the guest operating system, accelerated networking still appears as enabled, and the Mellanox driver is active and used for the VM NIC interface.
solution: 1.Enabling AN to avoid mismatch in configuration which can lead to OS instability or crashes:     2.Update driver version but keeping in mind below:   3.The latest driver can be downloaded from the following source: WinOF-2 / WinOF Drivers  4.If there is persistent incompatibility or driver-level instability, escalate to EEE Cloudnet via ICM

symptom:
Issue: Unable to connect to the AVD
session. Web client
error: Sign in failed. Please check your username and password and try again.  MSRDC client
error: "The sign-in method you're trying to use isn't allowed. Try a
different sign-in method or contact your system administrator."  Sign-in error
code: AADSTS50076  https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity-platform/reference-error-codes  Environment Windows 10/11 Per user MFA Disabled. Microsoft Entra joined session host VMs In this case Conditional access policy is configured to
require MFA
rootCause:
As user is attempting a authentication method that requires Multi-factor authentication. This error occurs when the user is expected to use
strong authentication method as per the MFA requirement from Conditional access
policy or per-user MFA.
solution:
Hence user can use strong authentication method
such as Windows Hello on local system (win 10 or later) to access RDP to Azure
VM. If you haven't deployed Windows
Hello for Business and if that isn't an option for
now, you can configure a Conditional Access policy that excludes the
Azure Windows VM Sign-In app from the list of cloud apps that require
MFA.

symptom:
The friendly name for assigned application groups in Azure
Virtual Desktop (AVD) is not being displayed correctly across various client
tools. Instead of the expected friendly name, users are seeing their alias as
the desktop name after assignment. This discrepancy is causing confusion and prevents
users from identifying their assigned application groups by the intended
friendly name.
rootCause:
Upon investigation, it was found that the customer is
explicitly setting the friendly name for the session host. For the user, the
session host was registered with Azure Virtual Desktop (AVD) at UTC 2025-04-02
10:22:50. Subsequently, at UTC 2025-04-02 10:34:48, an update management call
was made by some Automation using the service principal. This call
performed two actions: it assigned the session host to the user and set the
friendly name of the host. It appears that the customer has a process in
place where a virtual machine (VM) is created, followed by a call to assign the
VM to a user, which also sets the friendly name
solution:
If the customer does not want the friendly name to be set,
they need to set friendlyname == null in the input parameters for the
update session host call. This way, if the customer performs a "get"
operation before the automation sets the friendly name, they will not see it.
However, once the automation is run, the friendly name will be visible in the
"get" call. Step-by-Step Guide: 
 Identify
     the Automation Process: 
 
  Determine
      the automation process that is setting the friendly name for the session
      host. This is typically done using a service principal. 
 
 Locate
     the Update Management Call: 
 
  Find
      the specific update management call that assigns the session host to the
      user and sets the friendly name. This call will have an ActivityID. 
 
 Modify
     the Input Parameters: 
 
  In
      the input parameters for the update session host call, set friendlyname
      == null. This will prevent the friendly name from being set during the
      automation process. 
 
 Verify
     the Changes: 
 
  Perform
      a "get" operation before the automation runs to ensure that the
      friendly name is not set. 
  After
      the automation runs, perform another "get" operation to verify
      that the friendly name remains unset. 
 
 Test
     the Process: 
 
  Test
      the modified process to ensure that the session host is assigned to the
      user without setting the friendly name. 
 
 Monitor
     and Adjust: 
 
  Monitor
      the process to ensure that the friendly name is not being set
      inadvertently. 
  Make
      any necessary adjustments to the automation script or process to maintain
      the desired behavior. 
  By following these steps, the customer can
ensure that the friendly name is not set during the session host assignment
process   Another Workaround to that is:  If we use the below query, we notice that the friendly
name is not retrieved and hence the issue does not reflect with any client
tool.   $sessionHostParams = @{
   HostPoolName = 'HostPoolName'
   Name = 'SessionHostName'
   ResourceGroupName = 'ResourceGroupName'
}   Get-AzWvdSessionHost @sessionHostParams | FL Name,
AssignedUser, FriendlyName

of RDP Shortpath for managed networks using ICE/STUN in Azure Virtual Desktop
(AVD). They are looking to isolate the cause of an AVD connection problem with

environment using the existing managed network with minimal changes and is
unsure how to disable RDP Shortpath communication for ICE/STUN using Group
Policy (GPO) or other methods
rootCause:
Group
     Policy Limitations: The GPO settings can disable RDP Shortpath
     communication via port 3390/UDP but do not provide an option to disable
     STUN for managed networks    
 Automation
     and Host Pool Properties: The customer has already made changes
     to the host pool properties as described but needs further guidance on
     disabling RDP Shortpath for managed networks using ICE/STUN
solution:
Host
     Pool Network Properties: 
 
  Use
      the settings on the Host pool Network properties in the Azure Portal to
      manage RDP Short path configurations    
 Firewall
     Configuration: 
 
  Use
      the local firewall to block UDP internally if the goal is to disable RDP Short path for managed networks    
 Group
     Policy Configuration: 
 
  Set
      the policy locally to use TCP only for the required host    
 
  If

 Verification
     Process:

symptom:
The customer is encountering issues with Azure Virtual
Desktop (AVD) virtual machines (VMs) in their host pool. Specifically, the VMs
are going into an "Unavailable" health state while their power state
is "Running". The customer wants to set up Azure Monitor alerts to
trigger when the VM's health state is "Unavailable" while the power
state is "Running". However, the alerts are being triggered
erroneously, either when the VMs are shut down and deallocated or upon
startup
rootCause:
Incorrect
     Alert Trigger Configuration: The alerts are being created based
     on the VM object only via a Resource Graph query/alert trigger. This setup
     relies solely on the VM resource power state, which is causing the alerts
     to fire incorrectly   
 Lack
     of Combined Health State Check: The current alert configuration
     does not include a check against the Desktop Virtualization provider to
     verify the health state of the VM. This omission leads to alerts being
     triggered even when the VM is shutting down or starting up
solution:
Combine
     Alert Triggers: 
 
  Modify
      the alert configuration to combine the VM resource power state check with
      a health state check against the DesktopVirtualization provider. This can
      be achieved using a Resource Graph query that includes both
      conditions    
 Example
     Query: 
 
  Use
      the following Azure Resource Graph (ARG) query to identify VMs with an
      "Unavailable" health state while their power state is
      "Running": 
  arg("").desktopvirtualizationresources where type == "microsoft.desktopvirtualization/hostpools/sessionhosts" where properties.['status'] == "Unavailable"  This query can be combined with a similar query on the
compute resource to verify that the host itself is running or make it
conditional to fire only if the hosts go above the threshold set over a certain
number of minutes        3. Adjust
     Alert Conditions: 
 
  Ensure
      that the alert is set to trigger only when the VM is in a
      "Running" power state and the health state is
      "Unavailable". This adjustment will prevent erroneous alerts
      during VM shutdown or startup

symptom:
Users reported that while connected to an Azure Virtual Desktop (AVD)
session, they were able to copy and paste text content, but were unable to copy
and paste entire files or folders from their local device to the remote
session. This limitation affected file-based operations such as: 
 Drag-and-drop of files/folders from
     the local machine to the AVD session. 
 Using standard copy-paste shortcuts
     or context menu options for files.
rootCause:
This behavior was determined to be by design and is associated with
clipboard redirection restrictions within AVD environments. Key findings include: 
 Clipboard redirection applies to
     content within files (e.g., text or images) but not to the files
     themselves. 
 Attempts to copy an entire file
     (e.g., a .txt or image file) fail because the clipboard channel does not
     support file-level redirection. 
 This limitation is implemented
     intentionally to prevent security bypass attempts, such as renaming
     executable files to .txt to exploit redirection.
solution:
As an
alternate solution, you can transfer the files between local device to remote
session using RD webclient: Files can be successfully transferred between the local device and
remote AVD session using the Remote Desktop Web Client by following
these steps: 
 Sign in to the Remote
     Desktop Web Client and launch a remote session. 
 When prompted to access local resources,
     ensure that File transfer is enabled by checking the corresponding
     box and selecting Allow. 
 Once the session has started, open File
     Explorer and navigate to This PC. 
 Locate the Remote Desktop Virtual
     Drive on RDWebClient, which includes: 
 
  Uploads � for files
      uploaded from the local system. 
  Downloads � where
      files can be moved from the AVD session for download. 
 
 To download files from AVD to
     your local device, paste them into the Downloads folder. A
     prompt will appear asking for confirmation to download the files. Select Confirm
     to proceed. 
 To upload files, click the Upload
     new file (upward arrow icon) on the RD WebClient taskbar, and select
     the required files from the local system.  Notes: Ensure that "Drive Redirection" is not disabled, as it is
required for the Remote Desktop Virtual Drive to appear and function properly
within the session.

symptom: Issues configuring application groups. Receiving error: "No more role assignments can be created"
rootCause: We observed that customer organization had reached the limit of 4000 role assignments per subscription. This limit is considered a hard limit and cannot be increased on a per customer basis.
solution:
The recommendation from Microsoft is to reduce the number of role assignments on the subscription. The following documentation which outlines this limit and offers a handful of solutions that you can implement in order to reduce the number of role assignments.Troubleshoot
Azure RBAC limits - Azure RBAC | Microsoft Learn Understanding the RBAC Role Assignment Limit Azure has a hard limit of 4,000 role assignments per subscription, which includes all assignments made at various scopes (subscription, resource group, or resource level). How the Limit Works: Each role assignment counts toward the 4,000 limit, regardless of whether it's assigned to a single user, service principal, or group.  Assigning a role to one user counts as one assignment.  Assigning a role to a group�even if that group has 50 or more members�still counts as just one assignment.   This means that assigning roles directly to individual users can quickly consume the available quota. For example: Assigning a role to 100 users individually = 100 role assignments.  Assigning the same role to a single group with 100 users = 1 role assignment.   Recommendation: As highlighted in Microsoft documentation, the top recommendation for managing this limitation is to: Use group-based role assignments instead of assigning roles directly to individual users.   This not only helps avoid hitting the assignment limit but also simplifies access management and improves scalability.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#201779

symptom: The customer is facing issues with configuring UDP ShortPath for Azure Virtual Desktop (AVD) over ExpressRoute (ER) in a managed network environment. They want to ensure that internal users' UDP traffic always routes over ExpressRoute and does not leave over the public internet, while allowing public users to connect via UDP ShortPath over STUN. The current configuration at the host pool level does not directly address this use case
rootCause: The issue arises due to the way the ICE/STUN protocol evaluates multiple routes and selects the one with the lowest latency. In an office setup, if public network access is not desired, it should be restricted within the network. However, restricting access to TURN servers from the corporate network impacts other services like Microsoft Teams, which also rely on these servers.
solution: To resolve the UDP ShortPath over ER issue, follow these steps: Verify VPN Connection: Ensure that the VPN connection is available and provides the best route. If the VPN route is unavailable, connections will default to the public network. Restrict Public Network Access: In an office setup, ensure that public network access is restricted within the network to route internal users' UDP traffic through ER. Custom ShortPath Setup: Implement a custom ShortPath setup to control which routes are used. Admins can move users to separate host pools and apply specific controls tailored to their business needs. Monitor TURN Server Migration: Keep track of the migration of AVD to dedicated TURN servers at new IP ranges. Once AVD fully migrates to these new servers, ensure that the old IPs are not accessible from the corporate network to direct traffic through ER. By implementing these steps, you can ensure that internal users' UDP traffic routes through ER while allowing public users to connect via UDP ShortPath over STUN

rootCause: Conditional Access Issue: The problem is related to the Graph API call made by the blade to get All Users/Groups, which is affected by conditional access policies. Browser Session: Due to Single Sign-On (SSO) enabled, the user's browser session logged in with the Windows account in Edge browser. For Google Chrome, the Windows Account extension was used and allowed for incognito mode, which worked as expected.  Profile Association Error: In Edge, users cannot add a secondary account to the profile as it results in the error: "This email address is already associated with another profile." solution: Clear User Profile: Consider clearing the user profile and then recreating it by deleting the contents of �%LOCALAPPDATA%\Microsoft\Edge\User Data\Default�. Remove Extension: Temporarily remove the Forcepoint extension for testing purposes.  Browser Team Engagement: Engage the browser team to address the issue of adding multiple work profiles in Edge.  Registry Settings: Enable the CloudApAuth in registry settings for Chrome and Edge.  Use Different Browsers: Use Google Chrome with Windows Account extension in incognito mode as a workaround.

symptom: Azure Virtual Desktop (AVD) virtual machines (VMs) are experiencing power state issues and health status showing as "Unavailable."
rootCause: The problem can arise due to several reasons: Power State Issues: The VM might be turned off or not properly configured to stay in the "Running" state.  Health Status Unavailable: The AVD session host might not pass critical health checks, such as domain join checks or side-by-side stack listener health checks
solution: Verify VM Power State:                Ensure the VM is turned on and configured to stay in the "Running" state. You can use the Azure portal or Azure CLI to check and manage the VM's power state.         2. Check Health Status:                Use the Azure portal to review the health status of the AVD session hosts. Identify any failed health checks and follow the recommended steps to resolve them                        3. Configure Alerts:               Set up alerts in Azure Monitor to proactively notify you of any power state or health status issues. Use the following query to monitor both VM power state and session host health status: --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- union (     arg('').resources     | where type == "microsoft.compute/virtualmachines"     | extend machineStatus = properties.extended.instanceView.powerState.displayStatus ), (     arg("").desktopvirtualizationresources     | where type == "microsoft.desktopvirtualization/hostpools/sessionhosts"     | where properties.['status'] == "Unavailable"     | extend agentstatus = properties.status     | project name, agentstatus ) | project name, agentstatus  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------             4. Restart Remote Desktop Services:                  Restart the Remote Desktop Services on the affected VMs. This can help resolve connectivity issues and ensure the VMs are properly configured           5. Update Remote Desktop Client:                    Ensure you have the latest version of the Remote Desktop client installed. Sometimes, updating the client can resolve connectivity issues  ================================================================= Steps to Create an Alert Using the Above Query Access Azure Monitor:  Go to the Azure portal and navigate to Azure Monitor. Create a New Alert Rule:  Select Alerts from the Azure Monitor menu. Click on New alert rule. Define Scope:  Set the scope to the resource you want to monitor (e.g., your AVD VMs). Add Condition:  In the Condition tab, select Custom log search. Paste the above Kusto query into the query editor. Configure Alert Logic:  Define the logic for the alert. For example, you can set the alert to trigger when the query returns results indicating a VM is not in the "Running" state or a session host is "Unavailable". Set Up Action Group:  Create or select an existing action group to define how you want to be notified (e.g., email, SMS, webhook). Review and Create:  Review the alert rule settings and click Create alert rule.  By following these steps, you can effectively monitor and receive alerts for power state and health status issues in your Azure Virtual Desktop environment.

symptom: Users attempting to connect to an Azure Virtual Desktop (AVD) session are encountering an orchestration failure. The error originates from the orchistration process when attempting to add the user to the Remote Desktop Users group.Error Details - Error Source: RDAgent - Error Internal: False - Error Operation: AddUserToRDUGroup - Error Code: -2147467259 - Symbolic Error Code: ConnectionFailedAdTrustedRelationshipFailure - Error Message: - Exception of type 'Microsoft.RDInfra.RDAgent.Service.AddUserToLocalGroupAdTrustedRelationshipFailureException' was thrown. Configuration & Observations- The host pool is configured as a personal host pool with direct assignment. - Diagnostic activity logs indicate users are connecting to multiple session hosts.User Identity Validation:- Checked whether the user is an Entra ID user or an AD-created user synced to Entra ID.  Customer's Deployment Setup- DevOps Pipeline: Uses Terraform to deploy AVD environments.- Personal Host Pool: - New VMs are created daily as part of Privileged Access Workstation (PAW) machines using the DevOps pipeline.- Users access session hosts via the PAW application, which triggers a PowerShell script to assign the session host.
rootCause: Issue Analysis- Logs indicate failure in adding users to the Remote Desktop Users group when done via automation.- Manual assignment using the Azure Portal works flawlessly: - User logs off, removes the previous assignment, and assigns a new session host via the portal. - The user is successfully added to the Remote Desktop Users group, and connections work. - Key difference: Assignment done via PowerShell script (PAW automation) fails, while Azure Portal assignment succeeds.  Potential Root Causes- PowerShell Script or DevOps Pipeline Issue- The script may not have the required permissions or fail due to an Entra ID/AD trust issue.- Possible execution context problems�check whether the script runs under the correct identity. - Missing Group Membership Validation - Ensure the script correctly adds users to the Remote Desktop Users group with proper permissions. - Entra ID & AD Synchronization Delays - If the user is created in AD and synced to Entra ID, there might be a propagation delay causing failures.
solution: Recommended Fixes1.Validate PowerShell Script ExecutionRun the script manually on an affected session host:$hostPoolName = "YourHostPool"$resourceGroupName = "YourResourceGroup" $vmName = "Your-VM-Name" $aadUserPrincipalName = "user@domain.com"  New-AzWvdUserSessionHost `     -HostPoolName $hostPoolName `     -ResourceGroupName $resourceGroupName `     -SessionHostName "$vmName.yourdomain.com" `     -UserPrincipalName $aadUserPrincipalNameThis not under scope of Microsoft CSS if the customer is using Devops pileline to perform the action. 2. Check Group Membership ManuallyOn the affected session host, run:Get-LocalGroupMember -Group "Remote Desktop Users"- Ensure the user appears in the list. - If missing, try adding them manually:   Add-LocalGroupMember -Group "Remote Desktop Users" -Member "user@domain.com" - If manual addition works but script fails, review role-based access permissions. 3. Audit Entra ID & AD Sync- Check Azure AD Connect sync logs for delays/errors. - Run:    Get-MsolUser -UserPrincipalName "user@domain.com" - Ensure group memberships are updated in Entra ID.  The issue is likely rooted in PowerShell-based automation (PAW assignment) vs. Azure Portal manual assignment. Customer should check their powershell script that assgines user to session host.

symptom: Abstract  WindowsApp for iOS dose not recognize 106 Japanese Keyboard.   Symptoms When RDP (including AVD/Windows 365) session is connected from mac OS connected with Japanese 106 keyboard, Windows uses 101 English keyboard. ////// Mac OS Scenariomac OS send to Windows as Keyboard Layout = 0/KeyboardType = 4/KeyboardSubType=0 even if the Mac OS RDP Client has Japanese keyboard.0:028> kc  # Call Site 00 termsrv!CConnectionEx::MergeUserConfig 01 termsrv!CConnectionEx::InitializeClientData 02 termsrv!CRemoteTerminal::CRemoteTerminal 03 termsrv!CRemoteConnectionManager::GetInstanceOfRemoteTerminal 04 termsrv!CRCMConnDispatchManager::HandleConnection 05 termsrv!CConnectionEx::Accept 06 termsrv!CListenerEx::TransferWorkItem 07 termsrv!CListenerEx::staticTransferWorkItem 08 KERNEL32!BaseThreadInitThunk 09 ntdll!RtlUserThreadStart0:028> dt pClient key*Local var @ r11 Type _WINSTATIONCLIENTW*    +0x4f4 KeyboardLayout : 0    +0x4f8 KeyboardType : 4    +0x4fc KeyboardSubType : 0    +0x500 KeyboardFunctionKey : 0xc Windows (Winlogon.exe) checks the following registry HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Keyboard Layouts\00000411\Layouts File (REG_SZ) kbdjpn.dll Then, Winlogon.exe checks the following registry due to subtype = 0. HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Terminal Server\KeyboardType Mapping\JPN\00000000 (REG_SZ) kbd101.dll  Thus, Windows uses 101 English keyboard layout.  ///// In Windows Scenario, When mstsc.exe connect to the Windows,  mstsc.exe send to Windows as Keyboard Layout = 411/KeyboardType = 7/KeyboardSubType=2 if the Windows RDP Client has Japanese keyboard. 0:026> kc  # Call Site 00 termsrv!CConnectionEx::MergeUserConfig 01 termsrv!CConnectionEx::InitializeClientData 02 termsrv!CRemoteTerminal::CRemoteTerminal 03 termsrv!CRemoteConnectionManager::GetInstanceOfRemoteTerminal 04 termsrv!CRCMConnDispatchManager::HandleConnection 05 termsrv!CConnectionEx::Accept 06 termsrv!CListenerEx::TransferWorkItem 07 termsrv!CListenerEx::staticTransferWorkItem 08 KERNEL32!BaseThreadInitThunk 09 ntdll!RtlUserThreadStart 0:026> dt pClient key* Local var @ r11 Type _WINSTATIONCLIENTW*    +0x4f4 KeyboardLayout : 0xe0010411    +0x4f8 KeyboardType : 7    +0x4fc KeyboardSubType : 2    +0x500 KeyboardFunctionKey : 0xc  Windows (Winlogon.exe) checks the following registry (same as mac OS Client scenario) HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Keyboard Layouts\00000411\Layouts File (REG_SZ) kbdjpn.dll Then, Winlogon.exe checks the following registry due to subtype = 2. HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Terminal Server\KeyboardType Mapping\JPN\00000002 (REG_SZ) kbd106.dll  Thus, Windows uses 106 Japanese keyboard layout.
rootCause: From Bug 57670273: [iOS] WindowsApp for iOS dose not recognize 106 Japanese Keyboard. - Boards
solution: Change the following registry. HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Keyboard Layouts\00000411\Layouts File (REG_SZ) Default: kbdjpn.dll Changes to: kbd106.dll

symptom: You have an Windows AVD setup following these steps: https://dev.azure.com/Supportability/WindowsVirtualDesktop/_wiki/wikis/WindowsVirtualDesktop/1198769/Build-a-lab?anchor=step-4%3A-deploy-your-first-avd-host-pool-using-the-azure-portal We have a Domain controller and some hosts in Active Directory.  Then have MFA setup and SSO.  Have enablerdsaadauth:i:1 setting set in your AVD hostpool "rdp properties".  When connecting with a standard user using AAD authentication, all is working fine (you have the SSO experience).  When connecting with an administrative account, you get endlessly prompted for your AAD credentials (with authenticator validation succeeding). The sign-in is seen as "successful" in Entra. But you are prompted again.
rootCause: Administrative accounts are part of the protected groups (Administrators, Domain Admins, Domain Controllers, Backup Operators, Server Operators, Account Operators, Cert Publishers, Enterprise Admins, and Schema Admins).  By design admins (high priviledge accounts) cannot sign in to on prem resources as stated in :  FAQs for hybrid FIDO2 security key deployment - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn "The default security policy doesn't grant Microsoft Entra permission to sign high privilege accounts on to on-premises resources. Due to possible attack vectors from Microsoft Entra ID to Active Directory, it's not recommended to unblock these accounts by relaxing the Password Replication Policy of the computer object CN=AzureADKerberos,OU=Domain Controllers,<domain-DN>."
solution: Workaround 1: Do not set the enablerdsaadauth:i:1 setting in the rdp properties of the hostpool. This way, you will connect using Windows authentication and this will succeed even for an admin account.   Workaround 2: Remove the user from the Protected Groups.   Workaround 3 (just for test or to validate this is this issue): The default security policy doesn't grant Azure AD permission to sign high privilege accounts on to on-premises resources. To unblock the accounts, use Active Directory Users and Computers to modify the msDS-NeverRevealGroup property of the Azure AD Kerberos Computer object (CN=AzureADKerberos,OU=Domain Controllers,) on the domain controller machine:       After removal all works fine. But from a security point of view this should not be done. High priviledge accounts should not be removed from the msDS-NeverRevealGroup attribute.

symptom:
Customer is expecting
 a Data collection rule manually created by their platform team with the name -
 dcr_windows_avd. Notably this DCR is created by your platform team outside
 this workbook and is associated with the AVD VMs.
rootCause:
Customer was not
creating the DCR using the AVD insights documentation, suggested method is To
configure a DCR and select a Log Analytics workspace destination using the
configuration workbook. Also confirmed
with Product group, as per your request and got a suggestion that, The DCR
needs to follow our naming convention.The DCRs created in this workflow will have a fixed naming convention with a
prefix of Microsoft-avdi-followed by the region of the LA workspace. The
Configuration Workbook created DCR should be used to ensure that all required
data is collected for AVDI.
solution:
Recommendations As per the AVD
insights documentation, suggested method is To configure a DCR and select a Log
Analytics workspace destination using the configuration workbook:     1. From
the Azure Virtual Desktop overview page, select Host pools, then select
the pooled host pool you want to monitor. 2. From the
host pool overview page, select Insights, then select Open
Configuration Workbook. 3. Select the Session
host data settings tab in the configuration workbook. 4. For Workspace
destination, select the Log Analytics workspace you want to send
session host data to. 5. For DCR
resource group, select the resource group in which you want to create the
DCR. 6. Select Create
data collection rule to automatically configure the DCR using the
configuration workbook. This option only appears once you've selected a
workspace destination and a DCR resource group.  Ref: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-desktop/insights?tabs=monitor  Once a
Workspace destination has been chosen a DCR can be automatically created.    Note
that DCRs created in this workflow will have a fixed naming convention with a
prefix of Microsoft-avdi-followed by the region of the LA workspace. The
Configuration Workbook created DCR should be used to ensure that all required
data is collected for AVDI. Lab
test Please note the
DCR configured using the Configuration Workbook within the AVD Insights has the
DCR name which has a prefix of Microsoft-avdi" followed by the
region of the LA workspace. However, the DCR which your security team created
is missing the distinct prefix, which differentiates from other DCRs for other
Azure services.  Customer worked
with their Platform team to get the DCR created following these steps: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-desktop/insights?tabs=monitor#session-host-data-settings Permissions required
to create DCRs and DCR associations:  https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-monitor/essentials/data-collection-rule-create-edit?tabs=cli#permissions

symptom: After updating a Windows App, the following error prevents it from launching:Error message The service cannot be started, either because it is disabled or because it has no enabled devices associated with it.  The issue occurs for all users and persists even after reinstalling the app.
rootCause: This issue occurs when the Microsoft Account Sign-in Assistant service (`wlidsvc`) is set to [Disabled] as its startup type.
solution: Follow these steps to resolve the issue by setting the startup type of the service back to [Manual]: Sign in to the affected environment with a user account that has administrator privileges. Open the Services management tool by typing services.msc in the search bar and pressing Enter.Locate the Microsoft Account Sign-in Assistant service in the list and double-click it to open its properties.In the General tab, change the Startup type to [Manual].Click OK to apply the changes.Restart the operating system as a precaution.After restarting, try launching the Windows App and verify if the issue is resolved.

symptom: On Windows 11 23H2 and later and Windows Server 2025 platforms, including Azure Virtual Desktop (AVD) multi-users environments, the Windows Firewall security alert dialog may appear in the wrong user session. Generally, in the session where the Firewall dialog has been first requested. Impact: Affects all Windows SKUs and flavors. Reproducible internally and confirmed as a regression. Users may see firewall prompts associated with other users/sessions activities, causing confusion and potential security concerns.
rootCause: This issue occurs after the modernization of the firewall dialog UI introduced starting Win11 23H2 (Ni). The IFirewallNotificationDialog/IFirewallWarningDialog component is instantiated in session 0 (service context) without proper user impersonation. As a result, the dialog is not tied to the correct interactive user session.
solution: OS  Bug #  Resolving KB  Release Date  Comments  Windows 11 24H2/Windows Server 2025  59349596

symptom:
After upgrading an Azure VM from Windows 10 to Windows 11 multi-session OS, the ability to mount ISO files via right-click in File Explorer is no longer available.   This is due to a design change in Windows 11. On Windows 10 multi-session OS, standard users could right-click an ISO file in File Explorer and select Mount. On Windows 11 multi-session OS, the same action prompts for User Account Control (UAC), and standard users cannot mount ISO files directly.     This change affects Windows Server OS with RDSH role on Windows Server 2022 or later. The normal user will see below result with configurations:   Normal User can mount .iso file in the following condition is met.    

 
 
 
  Behaviour  
  Version of Windows OS 
 
 
 
  Never
  prompt UAC screen.
    This behavior can change via registry. 
  All version of client Windows OS Windows 10 multi-session OS on AVD   
 
 
 
  Prompt UAC screen.
    This behavior can change via registry. 
  All version of
  Windows Server without RDSH role   
 
 
 
  Always prompt UAC screen.
    This behavior cannot change via registry. 
  Windows
  11 multi-session OS on AVD Windows Server 2022 or later with RDSH role
rootCause:
Windows 11 multi-session OS introduced a security enhancement requiring UAC for ISO mounting operations.In Windows 10 multi-session OS, this behavior could be controlled via the following registry setting, which is no longer supported in Windows 11 multi-session OS or in Windows Server OS with RDSH role (Windows Server 2022 or later) Registry Key: HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\FsDepends\Parameters\AccessControlValue: ISOMountAllowNormalUserType: REG_DWORDData:0 : Restrict ISO mounting for standard users1 : Allow ISO mounting for standard usersBy default, ISOMountAllowNormalUser is set to 0 on Server OS and 1 on Client OS Chart of OS and registry combination for Normal Users. 

 
 
    
  Behaviour of
  ISOMountAllowNormalUser=0 
  Behaviour of
  ISOMountAllowNormalUser=1 
 
 
  Windows 10 multi-session OS
  on AVD All supported version of Windows Server without RDSH role   
  Cannot mount the .iso file
  and a UAC prompt appears. 
   Can mount the .iso file
  without a UAC prompt. 
 
 
  Windows 11 multi-session
  OS Windows Server 2022 or later with RDSH role   
  Cannot mount the .iso file
  and a UAC prompt appears. 
  Cannot mount the .iso file
  and a UAC prompt appears.
solution: On Windows 11 multi-session OS and Server OS with RDSH role, ISO mounting by standard users cannot be enabled through registry settings. UAC is mandatory.Workaround: Perform the mount operation using File Explorer with administrative privileges.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#206638

symptom: Abstract Several screen issues happen on the desktop on AVD (Azure Virtual Desktop) or Windows 365. Major Symptoms The mouse cursor doesn't show. The invalid white vertical lines show on the screen. The screen or mouse cursor is flicking.
rootCause: In many cases, the issue is with the display driver.However, sometimes the cause lies with the MPO (multiplane overlay). In this case, disabling the MPO might solve the issue. MPO Support - Windows drivers | Microsoft Learn
solution: Firstly, you should confirm whether the display driver is the latest version on the client machine on the client machine that is the device you are connecting from.If the display driver is *not* the latest version, please update to the latest version. If your issue isn't still fixed even though the latest version of the display driver, please set the registry below follow these steps on the client machine on the client machine that is the device you are connecting from. *Don't set the registry on the AVD or Windows 365 virtual machine. Open the registry editor (regedit.exe) Moe to HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\Dwm Click [Edit] menu. Select [New] -> [DWORD (32-bit) Value] Rename the value name from [New Value #1] to [OverlayTestMode] Double-Click [OverlayTestMode] Set the value data to "5" Push [OK] button Close the registry editor (regedit.exe) Reboot the client machine.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#206676

symptom: Introduction of AVD Diag Tool     AVD Diag Tool serves as a centralized Dashboard for examining AVD problems.      You can access the AVD Diag from https://aka.ms/avddiagtool   *If you do not have permission, please contact someone with Editor access to grant you the necessary permissions. *We would like to understand the number of users of this tool quantitatively in order to assess its impact. By accessing the tool via the shortlink we created above, we can count the number of accesses. Therefore, when using AVD Diag, please bookmark the shortlink and access the tool through it.      Training Video   The link below offers detailed instructions on how to use the AVD Diag Tool.  The below recording video shows AVD Diag tool overview and how to use with its demo. Introduction of AVD Diag  A Tool for AVD Investigation-20250808_002100UTC-Meeting Recording.mp4   Tutorial for Beginner     The following site allows you to experience the investigation process using the AVD Diag Tool by utilizing a tutorial prepared on the actual AVD Diag Tool, which guides you step by step through the operation checks.  AVD Diagnostics   Description   The following explains each feature displayed under "Activity Diagnostics" when launching the AVD Diag Tool.    Activity - Activities are displayed based on the information entered at the top of the page. You can refer to the displayed data and adjust the input at the top of the page if necessary.     Diag Error - Displays logs marked as errors within the Kusto Diag Activity.   Chart of ErrorCodeSymbolic - Shows a list of ErrorCodeSymbolic related to the specified information you filtered.   Error Internal Rate  - Displays the percentage of errors that include Internal Error among the shown errors.   RDP Shortpath or NOT - Indicates whether the connection is using RDP Shortpath.   Infra Trace - Displays information recorded in the Kusto RD Infra Trace table.   Client Trace - Displays information recorded in the Kusto RDClientTrace table.   Session Host Trace - Displays information recorded in the RDPCoreTSEventLog table, related to the Session Host.   Agent to Broker Heartbeat - Displays heartbeat information to confirm connectivity between the Session Host and the control plane.   FSLogix Trace - Displays information recorded in the RDFSLogixTrace table.   HostPool Info - Displays information related to the specified AVD environment and Host Pool.   Session Host Info - Displays information about the specified Session Host (ShoeboxAgentHealth data).   VM Operations - Displays operations performed on the specified Session Host. (Same as VM Operations shown in ASC.)  Deploy Error - Displays VM deployment errors for a specific Session Host.  Logon Delay - Shows the time taken to complete logon, broken down by each component.   Agent Update Failure Check - Displays information about failed agent updates.   TooManyRequests (429) - Displays the number of failed TokenRequest API calls due to TooManyRequests based on specified conditions. (If this spikes, it may indicate that the number of requests to VMs within a certain time frame has hit a limit.)  Estimated RTT/Bandwidth - Displays the Round Trip Time based on specified conditions  the time it takes for data to travel across the network and return.

symptom:
Customer has Azure monitor
for AVD set up and has LA queries configured to send email alerts as soon as
the VM goes unavailable. However customer has been
receiving false/positive alters post the configuration. Alert email has been
sent stating that the VM has been unavailable but the status of the actual VM
in AVD hostpool was always available.  There were no connection
issues/impacts reported during the mentioned time. There are no service restarts
or any other alarming errors on the VM event logs on the specified time. Kusto queries also confirm
that the VM has been available. It is confirmed that the VMs
status was available but LA is reporting the VM as unavailable.
rootCause: Expected behavior.
solution:
Resolution :   
 It is
     expected for AVD agent to disconnect & re-connect to the back AVD
     service momentarily for few secs. This is
     by-design and does not impact the production and moreover the VM would
     still show as available. However
     LA triggers an alert for this expected disconnect/re-connect.   
 
    To
avoid this, PG has suggested to change the LA query in way that it only alerts
VMs that have been in unavailable state for over 2mins. This would eliminate
the expected false positives alerts & provide an alert only when the VM is
really impacted & unavailable.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#206804

symptom: Users local Microsoft Teams calls disconnect shortly (within ~1020 seconds) after they launch a Remote VDI session. The problem is reproducible when the Teams account signed in locally differs from the Teams account inside the VDI. At the moment the VDI session starts, Teams in VDI initializes/optimizes media, and the local call drops.
rootCause: Competing Teams media stacks (local machine vs. VDIoptimized Teams) contend for HID/audio devices and WebRTC/optimization channels.  The account/tenant mismatch between local Teams and VDI Teams increases the chance that VDI media optimization or device redirection seizes control, causing the local call session to tear down.
solution:
Resolution 1  Disable Teams HID Manager (low risk, reversible) Mitigates device conflict by stopping Teams from actively managing HID peripherals when VDI starts. Registry (peruser): Path:  HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Teams\HID
Name:  DisableHidManagerV1
Type:  REG_DWORD
Value: 1
PowerShell (user context): PowerShell      New-Item -Path "HKCU:\Software\Microsoft\Teams\HID" -Force | Out-Null New-ItemProperty -Path "HKCU:\Software\Microsoft\Teams\HID" -Name "DisableHidManagerV1" -PropertyType DWord -Value 1 -Force | Out-Null                Deployment tip: Roll out via Intune (user script) or GPO (User Configuration  Preferences  Registry). Pilot first, then expand. Resolution 2  Prevent or Delay Teams AutoStart Inside VDI Avoids the collision during an active local call by not starting Teams in the VDI session immediately. Remove autostart entries (user context in VDI): PowerShell      $runKey = "HKCU:\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run"  # Classic Teams if (Test-Path "$runKey\com.squirrel.Teams.Teams") {   Remove-ItemProperty -Path $runKey -Name "com.squirrel.Teams.Teams" -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue }  # New Teams if (Test-Path "$runKey\com.microsoft.teams") {   Remove-ItemProperty -Path $runKey -Name "com.microsoft.teams" -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue }                 Set client preference Open Teams automatically = Off in the base profile or enforce via script/GPO. User guidance: Start/finish calls in one environment; avoid launching VDI midcall or launch VDI first and use the same environment for the call.  Validation & Rollout Pilot with a small user group:
Group A: Resolution 1 only. Group B: Resolution 1 + 2.   Test scenario: Start a local Teams call  launch VDI  observe for 2 minutes. Success criteria: The local call remains connected; no unexpected device flip or session drop. Scale: Use Intune/GPO for registry/policy deployment; bake service changes into the VDI gold image where applicable.

symptom: We have located an issue where Microsoft Runtime application dependency may become corrupt when 2 or more users log onto a Session Host and use that app. The issue occurs when a user logs off and breaks the Runtime so on next user logon, the app may fail Here is the scenario:  User A connects to AVD Session host launches 2 apps  ( app attach ( VHDX) that depend on Runtime 1.4 ( pre installed ) User B connects to AVD Session Host launches the same 2 apps that User A has launched User B closes the apps and logs off User A closes the app and logs off User B logs back on and now get the 2 apps greyed out on the start menu, and gets the following on App Launch       Check the Runtime  and its broken:      This is happening on 24H2 ( at the time of writing 23H2 and 25H2 have not been tested)
rootCause: During logoff, app attach in the RDAgent calls PackageManager.RemovePackageAsync() to deregister packages for the logged off user. This triggers automatic removal of framework dependencies. The removal operation enters "user profile changing" state while Windows/Appx is unloading the user profile.   When the profile becomes inaccessible mid-operation (because logoff could be complete), Appx fails to complete the removal and attempts to roll back. The rollback fails because it tries to restore state to the now-unloaded user profile, causing Appx to mark the framework package as "Modified, NeedsRemediation.  To summarize, it's a race condition between profile offloading during logoff and package deregistration.
solution:
Workarounds:   Block logoff until removal of package is done (impacts user experience) Stage the remediated package immediately if it goes into a bad state Instead of registering dependency package by full name, register package by family name. This would ensure Appx doesn't deregister/removes the dependency package upon logoff that causes this issue.    The other option for the customer is for the user to remove the dependency from their package manifest, but that requires repackaging and updating the package.  Preferred workaround:  There are 2 scripts available.   Create-FrameworkStagingTask.ps1
-  You can test this manually on a session host to ensure all is working. Run the script via Powershell ISE in elevated context. It will prompt you for the Runtime Location source, e.g c:\temp\Micrsoft.WindowsAppRuntime.msix. Enter the full path inc the file name, Once complete you will see    Check in Task Scheduler , the newly created Task appears:    Once you are happy , you can now deploy that
script via the other PowerShell script Deploy-FrameworkStagingTask-AzureVMs.ps1
to all session hosts in the HP:   You can do it via Azure CLI 
PowerShell   It will prompt you for the Resource
Group containing the Session Hosts   And then it will ask you for the
location of the runtime dependency which will be needed.       In my test I just copied the
Microsoft.WindowsAppRuntime to c:\temp\ on each of my 2 session hosts in a Temp
folder and specified that location ( as you can see above) or provide a share location containing the RunTime.  Check each session host that the scheduled task is created:      Please reach out to the content owner to access the scripts.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#207211

symptom: Unexpected hostname changes occurred on AVD session hosts at the operating system level, while the Azure VM resource names remained unchanged.    This caused confusion in the AVD host pool because the new names appeared there, even though the underlying VM names were intact. The behavior was not triggered by the AVD platform but likely by an Intune/device policy or manual user action inside the OS.
rootCause: Rename occurred inside the OS (not by AVD). Evidence includes NetSetup.log (NetpChangeMachineName), Event 1074 (planned shutdown), Event 6011 (name change), and Security Event 4688 indicating SystemPropertiesComputerName.exe was launched

symptom: When typing in Japanese in the Windows App on ChromeOS, two prediction or input suggestion windows are displayed simultaneously. This can cause confusion and make it difficult to select the correct input suggestion.
rootCause: Duplicate predictive input sources This issue occurs because both the source ChromeOS device and the destination AVD session host virtual machine or Windows 365 Cloud PC are providing predictive input. Each environment is running its own input method editor (IME) with predictive input enabled, which results in duplicate suggestion windows.
solution: Configure input settings to use only Microsoft IME on the destination To resolve the issue, configure your environment so that only the Microsoft IME predictive input on the destination AVD session host virtual machine or Windows 365 Cloud PC is used. Follow these steps: On Windows App: Go to General settings. Under Use scan code input when available, turn the toggle On.   On ChromeOS: Open Keyboard settings. Set the keyboard language to US.   On AVD or Windows 365 Cloud PC: Set the keyboard layout to Japanese.    Note The keyboard layout will operate as a US keyboard layout.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#207833

User
Unable to connect to AVD with Below Error.  "A user account restriction (for
example, a time-of-day restriction) is preventing you from logging on. For
assistance, contact your system administrator or technical
support.","Error code: 0xc07", "Extended error code:
0x600"   If we
check the activity id in Kusto these are Error code we could see.

rootCause:
User was
part of too many security groups.   When
same user tried connecting with direct RDP we got below error.   "During
a logon attempt, the user's security context accumulated too many security IDs.
Please try again or consult your system administrator."   The issue occurs
when the logon user is an explicit or transitive member of about 1,010 or more
security groups.  Logging on a user account fails - Windows Server | Microsoft Learn
solution:
Customer
removed the user from few unnecessary security group that fixed the issue.
